title: Echo Without a Voice
shortDescription: An investigative journalist receives precise anonymous threats referencing unpublished drafts. The sender accounts vanish within minutes. As her routines change, the messages adapt.

briefing.incidentSummary:
An investigative journalist reports receiving anonymous threats after publishing a sensitive exposé.
The messages arrive late at night, calm and precise.
There is no profanity, no panic, no rage.
Each message references details not yet published — draft notes, unreleased edits.

The sender accounts disappear minutes after each message.
No trace remains. No usernames repeat. No clear attacker appears.

The journalist begins altering her routines.
The messages adapt immediately.
Warnings become predictions.

You are assigned to determine whether this is random harassment, a personal stalker, or a calculated psychological operation — before the threats escalate into action.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify the single author behind the anonymous messages

› Analyze linguistic patterns, account metadata, and IP routing

› Collect at least 3 correct evidence items

› Flag the correct suspect to close the case and protect the journalist

suspects.name: Vikram Sen
suspects.profileNotes: Has a harassment history and uses burner accounts. However, his language is sloppy and emotionally volatile — the opposite of the controlled, professional tone in the threats. No timing discipline. Suspicion drops after Mini-Game #1 (linguistic analysis) confirms the author is trained and deliberate.

suspects.name: Maya Kulkarni

digitalFootprint.ipActivity: VPN rotation — 3 different countries per session
digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Same device fingerprint across all sessions

suspects.profileNotes: Former colleague with access to draft notes and unpublished edits during her time on the team. Professional tone, restrained language, matching sentence structures found in archived emails. Suspicion rises consistently across all three evidence phases. The key tell: her archived professional emails share exact cadence patterns with the threat messages.

suspects.name: NULL_VOICE

digitalFootprint.ipActivity: Heavy VPN rotation — multiple exit nodes

suspects.profileNotes: Initially appears to be the strongest technical suspect due to heavy VPN usage and obfuscated fingerprint. However, the anonymity infrastructure is over-engineered relative to the emotional intelligence in the messages. A person with this level of technical discipline would not reference personal details they have legitimate access to. Emotional mismatch. Suspicion weakens after IP analysis.

minigame.title: Linguistic Pattern Analysis
minigame.description: Compare sentence structure, cadence repetition, and emotional control across the three threat messages.
minigame.hint: Random harassers vary their language. A trained author uses consistent rhythm, sentence length, and emotional register.
hints: Count the words per sentence across all three messages. Is the variation natural?
Look at the emotional tone — calm, professional, controlled throughout. No escalation.
Compare the sentence endings — do they share a grammatical pattern not common in casual writing?

onSuccess.message: Account Metadata now accessible. Single deliberate author confirmed. Random troll theory eliminated.

items.label: Message 1 — 22:41: 'You still think hiding names protects you?'
items.detail: Sent at 22:41. No profanity. No rage. The sentence is a precise rhetorical question — not a taunt. References the journalist's source protection practice, which is known to colleagues but not public. Professional register suggests insider knowledge.

items.label: Message 2 — 22:44: 'You missed something in your last article.'
items.detail: References an omission in an article that was only visible in the draft version — not the published piece. The sender had access to unpublished editing notes. A different account, sent 3 minutes later, but same sentence cadence and professional tone.

items.label: Message 3 — 22:47: 'You should stop before someone gets hurt.'
items.detail: Third account, third message. The escalation pattern is controlled — each message increases pressure while maintaining the same calm register. No slang. Language mirrors professional email tone. Sentence structure identical to message 1.

minigame.title: Account Lifespan Correlation
minigame.description: Align the creation, first message, and deletion times of all three accounts. Detect the interval pattern and operational rhythm.
minigame.hint: Accounts created minutes before use and deleted within 10 minutes are not random — they follow a deployment script.
hints: Line up creation and deletion times. How long does each account live?
Each account lives for exactly 8–10 minutes. Is that a coincidence across three accounts?
The creation-to-first-message gap is always 2 minutes. The deletion follows 6 minutes after the message. This is scripted behavior.

onSuccess.message: IP Traces now accessible. Single operator confirmed. Accounts are deployed, used, and destroyed on a script.

items.label: Account Lifespan Pattern
items.detail: All three accounts follow an identical lifecycle: created 2 minutes before first message, deleted 6 minutes after. Zero personalization. Same obfuscated device fingerprint on all three. This is not three different people — it is one operator running a deployment routine.

items.label: Device Fingerprint Match
items.detail: Despite using different account names, IP addresses, and browser agents, all three accounts share the same obfuscated hardware fingerprint. The obfuscation was applied manually — but the underlying signature persisted.

items.label: VPN Rotation Pattern
items.detail: Each message uses a different VPN exit node — different country, different IP. However, the latency signature is identical across all three connections: 34–36ms response time. The underlying internet connection is the same. The VPN rotation is cosmetic, not structural.

rows.value: Same base connection — VPN is cosmetic rotation

items.label: 6-Minute Activity Window
items.detail: All three connections are active within a 6-minute window (22:41–22:47). The session ends abruptly at 22:49 — consistent with a scripted session manager, not a human manually disconnecting. The same 6-minute window appears in session logs from the prior week.

timeline.title: First Threat Sent
timeline.description: 'You still think hiding names protects you?' — references source protection.

timeline.title: Second Threat Sent
timeline.description: References omission in unpublished draft — insider knowledge confirmed.

timeline.title: Third Threat Sent
timeline.description: 'You should stop before someone gets hurt.' — controlled escalation.

timeline.title: All Accounts Deleted
timeline.description: Scripted deletion — all three accounts vanish simultaneously.

perfect.title: Stalker Identified
perfect.subtitle: Maya Kulkarni confirmed. Former colleague with draft access. Harassment stopped. Journalist protected.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Partially Resolved
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but insufficient evidence trail to formally prosecute.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Accusation
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real stalker was not the loudest suspect. Maya Kulkarni continues. The threats escalate.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: No evidence collected. The journalist remains at risk.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

aria.welcomeToHub:
...Signal acquired.
Someone is watching this journalist very closely.
The messages are calm. Precise. Personal.
That is not random. That is chosen.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Three panels. The answer is in the pattern, not the content.
Start with the chat logs — read the tone, not the words.
Something about the writing will feel familiar.
aria.viewEvidence:
No rage. No slang. No mistakes.
This is a person who chose every word.
Mark everything that sounds too controlled for a stranger.
aria.markEvidence:
Good. You're reading it right.
Now check the accounts — how long do they live?
A random troll doesn't delete on a schedule.
aria.decryptionHint:
The IPs rotate countries but the latency doesn't change.
The VPN is cosmetic. The connection underneath is the same.
Find the fingerprint that persists across all three accounts.
aria.flagSuspect:
You have the linguistic match, the device fingerprint, and the routing pattern.
One person. One script. One motive.
Who had access to those drafts?
